meta.setting: Ore trestles, timber loading frames, chute decks, tram tracks, and high work platforms above creek cuts
meta.rdr_clone_target: RDR-clone western with industrial height, sabotage, payroll pressure, and mine-road logistics

research_grounding.title: NPS Whiskeytown History and Culture
research_grounding.use: Mining, logging, and rugged transport pressures around Whiskeytown.

research_grounding.title: California State Parks Shasta State Historic Park
research_grounding.use: Gold-rush mining-town infrastructure and period industrial material.

generation_prompt.task: Generate the Ore Trestles region page for the 1899 Jefferson State western packet. This JSON contains shape and guidance only.
page_shape: H3 local marker line in the register of High Timber, Chute Dust, and Loose Bolt
square image reference immediately after the marker line
opening blockquote built as three short generated lines: trestle object, height or sound detail, plain table meaning
two compact paragraphs establishing ore trestles as loading sites, work hazards, sabotage targets, chase routes, and evidence points
closing blockquote that gives a generated trestle sign and practical table meaning
generation_guidance: Generate ore trestles as working industrial timber: raised rails, chutes, braces, loose bolts, spilled rock, warning shouts, and men who know which boards flex.
Give the table hooks around a stalled ore car, cut brake rope, missing payroll box, a fall nobody believes was accidental, a hidden witness below, or a company man demanding the line stay open.
Make height and noise matter; the page should guide cover, movement, falling hazards, visibility from below, and what gets drowned out by ore clatter.
Keep the craft rough and local: green timber repairs beside old sun-split beams, iron plates hammered flat, and dust so fine it gets into coffee.
generation_prompt.hand_cut_note: A trestle should feel like a job site that only stands because everyone is too poor to stop using it.
generation_prompt.table_spark: An ore-trestle scene should ask what is moving, what is loose, who is below, and who profits if the structure fails.
must_preserve: Use 1899 western mining, freight, and timber language only.
The generated page must include image path regions/images/ore-trestles.jpg.
The prompt must not contain final prose, final blockquotes, or finished field marks.
avoid: do not mention any prior packet, inherited taxonomy, or non-western source language
do not make the trestle a scenic overlook
do not use modern steel catwalks, safety rail systems, yellow hazard paint, electric hoists, or clean museum staging
self_check: The future markdown follows the exact heading sequence.
The future page is about ore trestles as work hazards, logistics points, and scene engines.
The prompt contains no finished page prose.
All language belongs to grounded 1899 Jefferson State western play.
